data.title: The Coming White-Collar Recession
data.description:
Today, we are going to time warp ourselves back 35 years. The year is 1990. The Berlin Wall had just fallen in November of the previous year. The long Cold War was rapidly coming to an end, and Americans were looking forward to spending the long-promised "peace dividend."

Globalization was soon put on steroids as the Cold War came to a close. NAFTA was signed at the very beginning of 1994, an agreement Ron Perot stated would cause a "giant sucking sound" as manufacturing jobs fled the United States. Something that in retrospect, seems at least prescient. This globalization wave accelerated even further as China was welcomed to the World Trade Organization in late 2001, a few months after 09/11.

What followed was a huge reduction in manufacturing employment across the United States. Much of the Midwest and other regions of the country like Pennsylvania were turned into the Rust Belt. The loss of millions of relatively high-paying blue-collar jobs is one of many factors driving increasing wealth inequality over recent decades in the U.S. and has also been a factor in increasing political polarization in the country.

Well, it seems history is not without an appreciation for irony. An inflection point is on the horizon that few are discussing. The prospects for the blue-collar workforce appear to be improving. The new administration is quite focused on reshoring manufacturing back to the States. Towards that end it has implemented the biggest hike in tariffs on imports in generations.

This is resulting in a huge and much-needed surge of tariff revenues into the U.S. Treasury. In addition, a rash of huge multinational companies have announced significant expansions to plans to add manufacturing capacity in the United States in 2025. A partial list follows below.

In addition, hundreds of billions of dollars are being allocated to build massive AI Data Centers for the likes of Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN), Meta Platforms, Inc. (META), Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL) and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT). This is creating jobs for tens of thousands of positions for construction workers, electricians, plumbers, carpenters, pipefitters, HVAC personnel, etc.

This huge construction boom should also significantly boost the economic prospects of states with access to low-cost and abundant natural gas supplies as this will be the primary source of delivering the massive amounts of electricity these facilities demand. This is why states like Texas, Pennsylvania and Louisiana have garnered huge new AI data center projects. This is also triggering a renaissance for the nuclear utility industry. A proposed new $25 billion AI data center in the panhandle of Texas could end up hosting the nation's largest nuclear energy site.

Then, there are large numbers of recent migrants who are leaving the country in 2025. Some 1.6 million of which have left the United States year to date, mostly via self-deportation. All things being equal, this should open up new blue-collar jobs in industries like home building, which has been heavily dependent on this labor source. These trends could provide a large boost to vocational education across the nation.

In contrast, the prospects for the white-collar workforce are noticeably dimming. The AI Revolution has a high likelihood of displacing workers at a faster rate than any paradigm shift in history. If AI delivers the productivity improvements projected, it will result in millions and millions of job losses. In addition, almost all of these job reductions will happen in the white-collar workforce.

Among the jobs most likely to be reduced or eliminated are sales and customer service representatives, entry-level research and financial analysts, legal and office assistants and even software programmers. A recent Federal Reserve Bank of New York survey found that 6.1% of computer science grads are out of work as are 7.5% of computer engineering grads. These are among the highest unemployment rates for all college majors.

For decades, much of the younger generation as well as displaced employees were told to "learn to code" to achieve job security. With the development of AI, that is no longer the case. It is now getting to the time of the year when corporate managements are starting to huddle to map out budgets and core priorities for 2026. How many of those planning sessions will be around major pushes to integrate more AI into operational and business processes? My guess is a high percentage, and those targeted productivity pushes will result in considerable job losses in 2026, in my opinion.

A recent small business blog survey offered up the following predictions (above). Another similar exercise in July had some of the following findings.

So, the $64,000 question for the economy and the markets is will new jobs be created fast enough to offset the massive job losses driven by AI in the years ahead? I am not one to doubt American ingenuity. However, it is hard for me to fathom new job creation being close to sufficient to replace job losses from AI in the years ahead if predictions come anywhere close to coming to fruition.

That means the unemployment rate is likely to tick up significantly in the coming quarters. This is going to particularly impact the younger generations of white-collar workers given that AI will significantly reduce entry-level positions. And this is a generation already struggling with massive student loan debt, whose payments have recently been restarted after a four-year taxpayer hiatus. Already, student loan delinquency rates are surging, recently hitting 12.9% and credit scores for millions of individuals with student loans are falling.

Accelerating white collar job losses, falling credit scores and rising delinquency rates are hardly supportive of demand for large-ticket items like vehicles and discretionary travel. It is also another headwind for the rapidly deteriorating housing market which I covered again in an article earlier this week.

If white collar jobs are displaced by AI and they cannot be replaced at nearly the same pace, it could trigger an overall recession in 2026 or 2027. It also could be the death knell for many office properties, one of several sub-sectors of the CRE space that are already struggling mightily.

data.title: Vanda: Next Phase Of Fanapt Growth Might Be With Bysanti Advancement
data.description:
Vanda Pharmaceuticals Overview

Vanda Pharmaceuticals Inc. (NASDAQ:VNDA) has several of its pipeline products approved for certain disorders. Such approved therapies that this company has are Fanapt, HETLIOZ, and PONVORY. The last time I wrote about this biotech was with a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Vanda Pharmaceuticals: FDA Approvals On Deck In 2024 Makes This A Must Watch". In this article, I noted a few catalysts that were approaching. In particular, I noted the opportunity for the company to expand its label of Fanapt for the treatment of patients with manic or mixed episodes associated with bipolar I disorder. I'm happy to report that the supplemental New Drug Application [sNDA] of this drug was granted, and this brings it within the scope to go beyond only being used to help treat patients with schizophrenia.

Another item I noted was its potential to receive U.S. FDA approval of tradipitant for the treatment of symptoms of patients with gastroparesis as well. However, on this front, a Complete Response Letter [CRL] of this drug for the treatment of these patients was issued. The main item that the FDA cited was that it needed to run two new studies. The company continues to pursue avenues of attempting to get this drug approved. One such action was for an opportunity of a hearing for the NDA of tradipitant. Speaking of this drug, the company also has a Prescription Drug User Fee Act [PDUFA] date of December 30, 2025, which is when the FDA will decide if tradipitant should be approved for the treatment of patients with motion sickness. Sales of Fanapt had increased in the most recent second quarter of 2025, and this was thanks to the sNDA that brought in the expansion of the label to target the bipolar I indication.

Speaking of which, Fanapt [iloperidone] is an antipsychotic that has been approved to treat patients with schizophrenia and bipolar I disorder. However, the company took this drug and then created a new chemical entity [NCE] active metabolite version of Fanapt, to be known as Bysanti [milsaperidone]. With this, it could provide the benefit of superior efficacy, but as an NCE, it provides the ability to extend patent life at the very same time. Speaking of this drug, there are two catalyst opportunities I will go over below; they are a PDUFA decision date and also an ongoing study program to develop this drug as an adjunctive treatment for patients with major depressive disorder [MDD].

Bysanti As An Improved Version Of Fanapt Could Further Carry The Torch

As I noted above, the company already had FDA approval of Fanapt to treat patients with acute bipolar I disorder and was able to obtain the sNDA to expand the label use to also go after patients with schizophrenia. Speaking of this drug, sales of Fanapt in the most recent Q2 of 2025 increased year-over-year by 27% to $29.3 million. Again, this was likely due to the label expansion of being able to treat bipolar I patients. Before going on further, the company is also looking to expand this drug further. How can this be possible? This would be to develop long-acting injectable [LAI] Fanapt to treat these patients. The use of an LAI version of this drug is being explored in an ongoing phase 3 study program. However, it is believed that such a version won't be available to patients until 2026. Beyond being able to help in regard to quality of life [QoL] for patients, the scope of this move is the potential to expand patient life. If such patents are granted an extension, then it could lead to exclusivity of the drug all the way out to 2040.

Getting back to the advancement of its active metabolite version of Fanapt [iloperidone], Bysanti [milsaperidone], it is expecting to be able to have this drug eventually approved to treat adult patients with acute bipolar I disorder plus schizophrenia and also those with major depressive disorder [MDD]. In terms of the use of Bysanti for acute bipolar I disorder plus schizophrenia, the FDA has set a PDUFA review date of February 21, 2026. This is the date by which the company could further expand its presence in these two neuropsychiatric conditions. The second opportunity involves the ability to expand this very same drug towards another patient population, which is those with major depressive disorder [MDD].

The goal is to use Bysanti as a once-per-day adjunctive therapy for these patients who have an inadequate response to their current therapy. Hopefully, the FDA will approve this drug since it is just another version of Fanapt, but this remains to be seen. In essence, when Bysanti [milsaperidone] is given orally, it interconverts to iloperidone. Clinical studies done by the company have shown that both iloperidone and milsaperidone, at high and low doses, have been shown to be bioequivalent. What does this mean? This means the drug works the same at the time of drug-site interaction. With this being said, the release of results from the ongoing trial exploring the use of Bysanti as a once-a-day adjunctive treatment for patients with MDD is expected in 2026.

Selective JAK Inhibitor Could Overcome Polycythemia Vera Treatment Therapies

Another development that happened for Vanda Pharmaceuticals is that it was given Orphan Drug Designation [ODD] from the FDA for one of its experimental therapies known as VGT1849B. This particular selective peptide nucleic-based JAK2 inhibitor is intended to be developed for the treatment of patients with polycythemia vera [PV], which is a disorder characterized by an overproduction of multiple cells in the body. There is an overproduction of red blood cells [RBCs], white blood cells [WBCs], and platelets. This is very bad news, because with an overabundance of these cells, it causes a person's blood to become thick. In turn, this leads to the development of several symptoms that these patients with PV must deal with, such as the following:

  • Fatigue.
  • Itchy skin.
  • Increased risk for bleeding episodes.
  • Enlarged spleen.

The worst risk of all is excessive RBCs that can cause clots in a person, as this can be deadly. Not only that, but if not properly treated, then it could also eventually, in rare cases lead to acute leukemia as well. This might be a good opportunity for Vanda Pharmaceuticals to go after because the market is expected to grow in the next several years. It is expected that the polycythemia vera market size will reach $2.03 billion by 2032. So how can a drug like VGT1849B work in treating these patients?

Well, as I noted before, this drug entity is a selective JAK2 inhibitor. The point of this is that it is believed that up to 95% of patients with PV harbor the JAK2 V617F mutation that leads to the production of JAK2. There are several JAK2 inhibitors that have already been approved to treat PV, and they are Jakafi, Inrebic, Ojjaara, and Vonjo. While these are ideal for treating this disorder, Vanda believes that it can make a bigger impact by using this JAK2 inhibitor and this is because of its selective nature. That is, it provides the mechanism of action [MOA] of JAK2 inhibition but at the same time doesn't produce off-target kinase effects. What this means, in essence, is that it could offer a drug for these PV patients with reduced toxicities and/or side effects. The point is not to target other JAK kinases or those in a similar family of kinases to produce unwanted toxicities. A recent update for this program is that Vanda Pharmaceuticals was given Orphan Drug Designation [ODD] for VGT-1849B for the treatment of these patients with PV. Lastly, with the way this company developed this drug, there is one other possible advantage it could offer because of its selective nature, and that is infrequent dosing.

VNDA Stock Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC filing, Vanda Pharmaceuticals had cash of $325.6 million as of June 30, 2025. The company has been continuing to grow sales, which is a good sign. In terms of one drug, Fanapt, it grew by 27% year-over-year to $29.3 million. Again, this was thanks to the addition of the label to include the targeting of bipolar I disorder. Thus, this label expansion really helped push this company to see an increase in sales. What I will say, though, is that this company has some work to do, which is to boost sales in other drugs like PONVORY.

This is because sales of this particular drug in the first six months of 2025 saw a decrease of 18% to $12.7 million compared to the year prior. Even HETLIOZ saw a 4% decrease during the 6-month 2025 time frame from the prior year. Again, the saving grace was Fanapt. Total revenues of the first 6 months of 2025 reached $102.6 million, which was a 5% increase compared to $97.9 million for the same time period in 2024. The truth is that the company doesn't have a lot of cash on hand, and this is based on the 10-Q SEC filing. The company believes that the cash on hand is only enough for it to operate its business for at least the next 12 months.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Vanda Pharmaceuticals. The first risk to consider would be in terms of the ongoing PDUFA review date by the FDA of February 21, 2026. This is the date by which this U.S. agency will decide whether or not Bysanti should be approved for the acute treatment of bipolar I disorder and schizophrenia patients. The risk here is that even though Bysanti is an active metabolite version of Fanapt, there is no assurance that the FDA is going to approve it to treat these neuropsychiatric disorder patients.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the development of Bysanti for potentially another neuropsychiatric disorder, which is that of major depressive disorder. It is currently in the process of evaluating a phase 3 study using this drug as an adjunctive therapy to treat patients with MDD. It is expected that results from this late-stage trial will be released in 2026. The risk here is that even though Bysanti has been shown to work in the targeting of patients with acute bipolar I disorder and schizophrenia, there is no assurance that it is going to help treat these patients with MDD. Especially the nature of these patients, who are those who don't achieve an adequate response on their current therapy.

The third risk to consider would be in terms of the NDA of tradipitant, which is being reviewed by the FDA as a potential treatment option for motion sickness. The FDA had set a PDUFA date of December 30, 2025, to review whether or not this drug should be approved for motion sickness. The risk here is that even though the NDA was accepted and a PDUFA date had been set, there is no assurance that Vanda Pharmaceuticals will be granted U.S. marketing approval for it.

The fourth and final risk to consider would be in terms of the financial position that this company finds itself in. The reason is because, as I noted above from its 10-Q SEC filing, it believes its cash on hand of $325.6 million is only going to be enough to allow it to fund its operations for at least the next 12 months. The risk here is that the company doesn't have a good amount of cash on hand to fund its operations for an extended period of time. In my opinion, it is likely going to need to raise cash at least at some point during the 2nd half of 2025. It is not solely based on the cash projection that it has set forth, but with two upcoming PDUFA dates set, it may need to have additional cash on hand to further its commercialization efforts.

Conclusion

Vanda Pharmaceuticals has done pretty well in terms of continuing to grow its revenue. As I stated above in this article, it still has a long way to go to see substantial growth in revenues. Especially since its drugs HETLIOZ and PONVORY saw a decline in the first 6 months of 2025, compared to the same time period in the prior year. The saving grace was that of Fanapt, whereby sales of this particular drug increased by 21% to $52.8 million in the first 6 months of 2025, compared to the same time period in the prior year. I believe that the approval of Bysanti could further its presence in the acute treatment of bipolar I disorder and schizophrenia.

Along with the fact that there is an ongoing phase 3 study using this drug to target patients with MDD, the significance of obtaining positive results from this study would be huge because this is a large target-market indication. The global major depressive disorder market is expected to reach $11.56 billion by 2032. It remains to be seen, though, if the company can effectively use Bysanti as an adjunctive treatment for these neuropsychiatric patients, but the potential is there. The last time I spoke about this biotech, I had a "Buy" rating. I believe that this stock should be kept at a "Buy" rating because it has bullish prospects in place. Along with the fact that it was able to expand the label of Fanapt to include that of bipolar I disorder patients.

data.title: PYLD: Diversified Bond ETF, Below-Average Volatility, Above-Average Dividend Yield
data.description:
The PIMCO Multisector Bond Active Exchange-Traded Fund (NYSEARCA:PYLD) is a diversified bond ETF with a strong focus on MBS and other securitized assets. PYLD is quite similar to several other ETFs I've recently covered, including the Angel Oak Income ETF (CARY) and the Janus Henderson Securitized Income ETF (JSI). It is slightly riskier than these, with higher potential returns, higher returns in the past, and an average 5.9% dividend yield. PYLD is a strong investment opportunity and a buy, similar to its peers.

Differences to Bond Indexes

As PYLD is similar to several ETFs I've recently covered, I thought to write this article emphasizing the fund's differences from its peers and to broader bond indexes. I have a more in-depth overview of the fund itself here, but I still look through all the important fundamentals in this article.

Credit Quality

PYLD focuses on securities without credit ratings, with these accounting for 76% of its portfolio. Percentages for most bond index ETFs are close to zero/low single-digits. Credit quality seems reasonable enough, as PYLD focuses on MBS and assorted securitized products, which tend to have good credit quality. Credit spreads spiked this past April, with PYLD seeing a small 2.5% drawdown, similar to investment-grade corporate bond indexes. There is no evidence that these unrated securities are excessively risky.

Rate Risk

PYLD has a duration of 4.4 years, compared to 5.9 for BND and AGG, both broad-based bond index ETFs. Duration relative to treasuries, investment-grade corporate bonds, and most bond sub-asset classes is lower, too.

PYLD's below-average duration means below-average rate exposure, gains when rates go down, and losses when these decrease. PYLD missed most of the past hiking cycle, but it did experience a (temporary) rate spike during late 2023. Losses were below-average, as expected.

Dividend Yield and Returns

PYLD has a 5.9% dividend yield, higher than most investment-grade and corporate bond indexes but lower than high-yield corporate bonds.

PYLD's returns are slightly lower than those of high-yield corporate bonds, higher than investment-grade and corporate bond indexes. Some combination of a higher yield, different asset mix, and (potentially) alpha and leverage explains these results.

PYLD compares quite favorably to short-term bond indexes, significantly outperforming those focusing on investment-grade securities since inception, marginally underperforming riskier high-yield ones. PYLD underperformed during the second half of 2023, though, and I'm not sure what explains these results.

Differences to Peers

PYLD is quite similar to several ETFs I've recently covered, including BINC, CARY, FLXR, JPIE, and CGMS. As these ETFs have somewhat similar characteristics, I thought to do a quick comparison between them, focusing on PYLD and what sets it apart from its peers. Doing so should help investors decide if PYLD is the right ETF for them.

Portfolios

PYLD focuses on MBS and assorted securitized investments, as is quite common in this niche. Lots of exceptions, though, with BINC having a much broader, balanced portfolio:

CGMS focusing on corporate bonds:

Credit Quality

PYLD focuses on securities without credit ratings. The fund's peers are lean investment-grade, with sizable investments in high-yield investments and smaller investments in those without credit ratings. The uncertainty here is something of a negative itself and increases risks too.

As mentioned previously, and notwithstanding the above, PYLD's overall credit quality seems to be reasonably good. Performance seems good too, with the fund seeing about average losses this past April, in which spreads temporarily spiked. Looking at the fund's performance during a longer-lasting downturn would have been ideal, but this is the only adverse market event that the fund has experienced.

Duration

PYLD has the highest duration in its peer group, although the differences here are rarely significant:

Due to the above, PYLD should see higher gains when rates decrease and higher losses when these increase. Although there have been no significant, long-lasting rate movements since PYLD's inception, there have been a couple of smaller ones. Rates rose during some later months in 2023, with PYLD seeing above-average losses. Figures here do not closely track duration and are strongly influenced by volatility/small differences in portfolios.

Although PYLD should see slightly higher gains than peers if rates plummet from here on out, other factors matter too and could end up mattering more. Dovish investors might prefer alternatives to PYLD, but, again, the differences here are small, and other factors might end up mattering more.

Overall Risk and Volatility

PYLD seems marginally more volatile than its peer average. Differences here are small and fluctuate month to month.

In my opinion, and considering the above and PYLD's apparent leverage, the fund is slightly riskier than its peers. Small difference though.

Of its peers, JPIE is generally the least volatile, BINC the most diversified. Both might be of greater interest to more risk-averse investors than PYLD.

Dividend Yield and Returns

PYLD's 5.9% dividend yield is about average:

Returns are marginally above-average, but they vary depending on the time period selected. As the different ETFs have different inception dates, results vary depending on the ETFs selected. Returns below exclude FLXR, the best-performing ETF here, as it's the youngest one too, and the longer time frame seems more relevant.

PYLD has more consistently outperformed its peers in the recent past, including YTD. Returns remain quite similar between these ETFs, though.

In my opinion, and considering the broader macro context and prior coverage, PYLD's overall performance track record is the strongest in its peer group. As the differences here are small and the data is messy, it is difficult to say for certain.

As an aside, every now and then, I think to write my articles like this one: emphasizing what sets the fund apart from the index and from its peers. I think that by emphasizing the differences, the investment thesis, benefits, and downsides of an investment become more apparent, although I'm not sure that the articles ever turn out as originally envisioned by me.

Conclusion

PYLD is a diversified bond ETF with a strong focus on MBS and securitized investments. It is a strong investment opportunity and a buy.

data.title: Commercial Bancgroup Begins $173 Million IPO Rollout
data.description:
CBK’s Revenue Growth Has Slowed In 2025

Commercial Bancgroup, Inc. (CBK) has filed to raise $172.5 million in an IPO of its common stock, according to SEC S-1 registration information.

CBK operates a community bank network in Kentucky, Tennessee and North Carolina.

The company's revenue growth has slowed in the first half of 2025, and so has profit growth.

I’ll provide an update when we learn more IPO details from management.

What Does CBK Do?

Harrogate, Tennessee-based Commercial Bancgroup, Inc. was founded to provide a wide range of banking services, loans and other financial management functions for individuals and commercial entities in multiple states.

Management is led by President and CEO Mr. Terry L. Lee, who has been with the firm since 1989 and was originally hired at the bank as a bookkeeper and has assumed positions of increasing responsibility since.

The company's primary offerings include:

  • Real estate loans.

  • Commercial loans.

  • Consumer loans.

  • Deposit services.

  • Other services.

Most of the company's revenue is driven by real estate loans, as the pie chart breakdown indicates here:

The firm's current operating footprint is shown in the map below:

CBK has booked a fair market value investment of $8.5 million as of June 30, 2025, from investors.

Salaries & Employee Benefits expenses as a percentage of total revenue have dropped as revenues have grown, as the figures below indicate:

What Is CBK’s Market?

According to a 2025 market research report by the Mortgage Bankers Association, the U.S. commercial real estate lending market has been a dynamic market during and after the pandemic, with total volumes rising, falling and then rising again, as the chart shows below:

The group forecasted continued growth in 2025, with an expected 16% gain in origination volume compared to 2024.

Also, the report stated that "financial market volatility and economic uncertainty certainly put this number at risk."

The firm competes against a wide variety of financial companies, including other local, regional or national banks, credit unions, mortgage companies, trust companies, brokerage firms, consumer finance companies and other financial intermediaries.

CBK seeks new deposit customers and loans from primarily small and medium-sized businesses in its coverage areas through its in-house efforts, media outreach and referrals.

As of June 30, 2025, total assets were $2.26 billion, with net loans of $1.774 billion, representing a CAGR of 8% from 2020 to 2H 2025.

I’ve prepared a thumbnail SWOT analysis for CBK:

Strengths

  • A history of successful acquisitions to expand into new markets, a scalable decentralized operating model, and long-standing customer relationships provide the core of its deposit base.

Weaknesses

  • The concentration of the loan portfolio in commercial real estate has a ‘Needs to Improve’ rating under the Community Reinvestment Act, which may limit its operations and strategic options.

Opportunities

  • Growth potential in high-growth MSAs, with a focus on small and medium-sized business opportunities.

Threats

  • Interest rate volatility, changing regulatory environment.

CBK’s Recent Financial Results

The firm’s recent financial performance is summarized as shown below:

  • Slightly growing total revenue.

  • Higher net interest income after credit losses.

  • Growing net income and cash flow from operations.

As of June 30, 2025, CBK had $151.3 million in cash and $2.0 billion in total liabilities.

The company has generated free cash flow for the twelve months ended June 30, 2025, of $42.2 million.

CBK’s IPO Plan

CBK intends to sell shares of common stock in an IPO that may reach $173 million in size.

No new or existing shareholders have indicated an interest in acquiring additional shares of the IPO, at least as detailed in the initial prospectus.

Company, shareholder and executive lock-ups will be for the typical 180 days after the effective date of the prospectus, although there is an exception for holders of less than 5% of total shares.

According to CBK’s most recent regulatory filing, it plans to use the net proceeds as follows:

We intend to use the net proceeds from this offering to repay in full the outstanding indebtedness under the Community Trust Loan Agreement and to redeem in full our outstanding Subordinated Debentures and related Trust Preferred Securities, and to use the remaining net proceeds, if any, for general corporate purposes.

As of June 30, 2025, we had approximately $20.7 million of outstanding indebtedness under the Community Trust Loan Agreement.

(Source: SEC).

The firm’s presentation for the company roadshow is not yet available.

Pertaining to potential legal exposure, leadership said that no legal claims would have a material adverse effect on its financial condition or business operations.

The sole listed underwriter of the IPO is Hovde Group.

CBK's Revenue Growth Has Slowed in 2025

CBK is seeking U.S. public capital market access to pay down debt and for its general corporate growth plans.

The firm’s financials have produced only slightly growing total revenue most recently, growing net interest income after credit losses and higher net income and cash flow from operations.

Free cash flow for the twelve months ended June 30, 2025, was $42.2 million.

Salaries & Employee Benefits expenses as a percentage of total revenue have fallen as revenue has increased slightly, but its Salaries & Employee Benefits efficiency multiple fell to only 0.0x in the most recent reporting period as revenue growth fell to nearly 0.

The firm currently plans to pay dividends as and when the Board of Directors determines to do so, but expects not to pay dividends during its first year as a public company.

CBK’s recent capital spending history shows it has spent little on capital expenditures as a percentage of its operating cash flow.

The firm’s growth strategy features selective acquisitions to expand its footprint and organic growth efforts from within its existing coverage area.

Management expects to expand its activities in the greater Charlotte, NC, area in 2026.

The market opportunity for providing banking and lending services in the mid-Atlantic is large but subject to strong competition and moderate growth prospects.

Risks to the company’s outlook as a public firm include its "smaller reporting company’ and "emerging growth company" status, which may enable management to provide less information to shareholders.

CBK has concentration risks with its loans to the commercial real estate industry as well as regionally within the tri-state area of Kentucky, Tennessee and North Carolina.

Disclosed related party transactions are of de minimis value.

When we learn the bank’s proposed pricing and valuation assumptions for the IPO, I’ll provide a final opinion.

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced

data.title: Innodata: Price Pullback Doesn't Solve Growth And Concentration Risk
data.description:
I covered Innodata Inc. (NASDAQ:INOD) back in July. At the time, I rated the stock a hold as I had some concerns. My concerns were surrounding customer concentration, lack of scalability, margins, and potential slowdown in growth. Since I wrote my article, the stock has dropped just over 22%. It was the right call to avoid the stock at the time. The company has released earnings since I last reviewed the stock. With the drop in the stock price and another quarterly report of progress, I thought it would warrant another review of the stock.

Company Overview and Investment Thesis

I did a more thorough overview of the company and its operations in my previous article. I am not going to go in-depth here on the company and its operations. In short, the company has been able to capitalize on the AI movement. The company historically collected and annotated data at scale. Now they are using that data to train AI models. Innodata operates these functions under its Digital Data Solutions business segment.

The company's operations, or better, the industry in which it operates, are what first drew me to review the stock. Innodata is operating in a rapidly expanding industry. Companies are continuing to plow cash into AI. Innodata has found itself in the middle of the boom. They provide a critical function in providing data and training models. The AI model will only be as good as the data it is provided. The company has been able to win some clients and grow revenues, proving they actually provide value within the space. They are not mere speculation, but they are winning contracts and generating revenue.

Risks and Updates

I previously rated the stock a hold due to some concerns that I had. I want to review some of these concerns and update them based on the most recent quarterly report. See if the company has progressed or resolved the concerns that I had. Or at least done so to the point that I think it is a good investment.

Customer Concentration

One of my major concerns was customer concentration. The company derived 61% of revenues from a single customer during Q1 2025. During Q2, that number did decrease slightly to 58% of revenues. The decline mostly came due to a decline in revenue from that customer and not so much growth from other customers. I don’t expect that number to drop to nothing immediately, but I hoped the company was going to get a bit more revenue from other clients during the quarter and decrease the concentration risk.

The company did announce during the quarterly call that they did sign on a new customer, which they forecast to bring in $10 million in revenue during the second half of the year. That is a much-needed win, but it is still rather small. That would be $5 million a quarter in revenue, making up around 8.5% of revenues (based upon the topline number from Q2). This is a good win, but not enough to move the needle. Innodata needs a few more of those before it can reduce the reliance on a single customer and help secure growth going forward.

In the conference call, the company mentioned that they are continuing to have discussions with some larger potential clients. The company stated the following in the earnings call: “We have additional opportunities in the pipeline that, while not yet included in our forecast, appear reasonably likely”. These are still speculations, and nothing is set, at least not enough to forecast. That being said, the company is seeing potential future contracts. It is a matter of the company closing some of those opportunities.

Revenue Growth

One of my other concerns was the lack of revenue growth in the second half of the year. First, let's look at the results from Q2. The company reported revenues of $58.4 million, 79% year-over-year growth. A strong growth rate for sure. Sequentially, the revenue is barely up from $58.3 million in Q1. The company did raise its guidance from 40% growth for the year to 45% growth. That is an additional $8.5 million in revenue from the original estimate. I think the $10 million contract is the most likely contributor to this increase.

These all seem to be good growth rates, but in reality, the company is looking at slowing growth in the back half of the year. This stems from the concentration issue above. The company signed a big client in 2024, with Q3 of 2024 being the first full quarter of revenue with that major client. The company will lap that coming quarter, making it much harder for Innodata to show growth.

At a 45% growth rate, the company is expecting $247 million in revenue; that would be an average of $65 million for Q3 and Q4. That would be growth of 25% and 10% for Q3 and Q4, respectively. Still good growth, but a steep slowdown from what the company has been seeing. Also, it changes the valuation and what an investor would be willing to pay for the company.

My concern is also highlighted in the revenue for the DDS segment from Q1 to Q2. Revenues actually declined from $50.8 to $50.6 million within the segment. This has been the revenue driver for the company. It is also the most profitable segment. The company made up for the slight slowdown by growing revenues in the Synodex and Agility segments. These are both still small in terms of revenue, making up 3.5% and 9.6% of revenues. These segments would need significant growth in order to meet the needs of the company overall.

The company did come out and raise guidance from 40% to 45% revenue growth for the year. This makes the back half of the year look much better in terms of growth rates. That being said, it is not quite enough to alleviate my concern about revenue growth going forward. The company needs to win a few more contracts before it can show sustainable growth. Right now, it has not proven that to me.

Scaling and Margins

The other concern I had was related to margins. The company is not a software company that can just bring on another client to its platform. It is much more manual than that. It operates on a project basis. This makes it much harder to improve gross margin by scaling the business. The company did not really grow revenues from last quarter, so it is hard to see if the company will be able to increase margins with more scale. In terms of margin, the GM dropped slightly from Q1 to Q2, with GM sitting right around 39.5%. The company was able to decrease its SG&A costs and overall increase its operating margin by 100 basis points for the quarter.

It is good to see the company being prudent and controlling operating costs in order to improve profitability. My concern on the margins still remains. The GM declined from quarter to quarter. Revenues did not see much of an increase, so it's hard to know if scale will improve margins. So for now, it remains a concern for me. Until I can see more automation integrated into the business, the margins will remain a concern for me as the company scales.

Price and Valuation

The stock price fell 22+% since I wrote my article. This changes the valuation of the company. I noted previously that the company has been prone to price fluctuations, and we saw that in the last month. The company has a Fwd PE ratio of 51.4 for 2025 and 38.2 for 2026. This is based upon expected revenue growth of 45% for 2025 and another 20.5% for 2026. Based on what we are seeing from the company in terms of growth estimates for the second half of this year, 20% might be a lofty goal to achieve for the company. Either way, even following the stock price drop, the company still trades at a fairly high valuation. If the company is growing revenues at the 10% rate that it expects to end the year, then the stock price does not warrant that type of multiple on its earnings, in my opinion.

Conclusion

Innodata has seen its stock price drop 22% since I last reviewed the company. That is a pretty good price decline. This decline came on the back of earnings. I think there was profit-taking as the company has seen a strong run-up in price. I think that this was also due to the same concerns I had originally, which were not alleviated much. The company still has a strong concentration risk, with one client making up nearly 60% of revenues. The company did raise guidance for the year, but it is still looking at a steep drop-off in revenue growth. Even following the drop, the stock remains at a high valuation.

The company continues to show potential. It has been shown that it has a place in the fast-growing AI industry. The concerns I had still remain, so I maintain my hold rating. The drop in the stock price makes it more compelling, but I still want to see more advancement on some of my concerns. I will continue to monitor the stock.

data.title: HDEF: Ex-U.S. ETF With Cheap Fees, Good Value, And Average Return
data.description:
This article updates my review of HDEF published in February 2024 in light of current holdings and recent performance.

HDEF strategy

Xtrackers MSCI EAFE High Dividend Yield Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:HDEF) was launched on 8/12/2015 and tracks the MSCI EAFE High Dividend Yield Index. HDEF has a portfolio of 111 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 4.08%, a trailing 12-month yield of 3.87% and a total expense ratio of 0.09%. Distributions are paid quarterly.

As described by MSCI, the methodology starts with the MSCI EAFE Index, a developed market benchmark excluding the U.S. and Canada. To be eligible, stocks must have above-par dividend yields and quality characteristics that are both sustainable and persistent. The index applies quality screens to exclude stocks with deteriorating fundamentals that may force them to reduce dividends.

The index is weighted based on free float-adjusted market capitalization. Additionally, the fund may hold derivatives that can help track the underlying index. The portfolio turnover rate was 26% in the most recent fiscal year. This article will use as a benchmark the parent index MSCI EAFE Index, represented by iShares MSCI EAFE ETF (EFA).

Portfolio

HDEF is mostly invested in large and mega-cap companies (about 92% of asset value). About 77% of assets are invested in Europe and 21% in Asia. The heaviest country in the portfolio is Switzerland (19.2%), followed by the U.K. (15%) and France (14.1%). Other countries are below 8%. The next chart lists the top 14 countries, representing an aggregate weight of 95.5%. Hong Kong is at 2.3%, so direct exposure to geopolitical risks related to China is low. Compared to the benchmark, the fund mostly overweights Switzerland and downplays Japan.

The portfolio has a focus on financials (26.7%) and significant exposure in consumer staples, healthcare and energy (12% to 15%). Other sectors are below 10%.

The portfolio is quite concentrated, but risks related to individual companies are moderate. Indeed, the top 10 holdings, listed below, represent 42.4% of asset value, and the heaviest position weighs 5.3%.

Takeaway

Xtrackers MSCI EAFE High Dividend Yield Equity ETF (HDEF) offers a 4% yield from ex-US dividend stocks with above-average quality characteristics. HDEF has cheap fees, good value characteristics and is well-diversified geographically, but quite concentrated in financials and in its top 10 holdings. Regarding performance, HDEF is close to its parent index since inception, and average among competitors over the last 9 years. In particular, HDEF has underperformed FNDF, but it may be preferred by dividend-oriented investors for its higher yield and lower volatility.

data.title: Silicon Motion: Execution Outpaces Expectations As MonTitan And Mobile Drive Tactical Upside
data.description:
Back in June, I laid out a few major reasons I was bullish on Silicon Motion Technology Corporation (NASDAQ:SIMO), and one, sector leadership in a growing market, was the main standout. None of that’s changed, and in fact, a couple of those levers look stronger. For example, PCIe 5.0 controllers now make up about 10% of the company’s client SSD revenue, well ahead of anything I could have expected.

Additionally, MonTitan has moved from being a promising technology to actively ramping with Tier 1s, and eMMC/UFS got a solid shot in the arm from China OEM restocking. We can also mix in firmer NAND pricing and leaner inventories, and the backdrop is better than I thought it would be by this point in the year.

Consequently, I’m keeping my call at a Buy because the risk/reward tilts positive here. The execution we’ve just seen gives me more confidence in the durability of this rebound, even though this is still a cyclical name that will ebb and flow with NAND pricing and enterprise ramps.

A Snapshot of Q2 2025’s Numbers

With Q2 2025’s numbers, the rebound showed up earlier than I penciled in. Revenue hit $198.7 million, up 19% from Q1. However, it was a 6% drop from the same period in the previous year. Gross margin moved up to nearly 2 percentage points YoY to 47.7%, and the company’s non‑GAAP operating margin climbed to 12.8% from 8.9% in Q1. That’s more operating leverage than I expected this soon, and it tells me management is actively improving the mix and controlling costs.

So, What’s Driving The Bullishness?

When I wrapped up my June piece, I made the case that the real proof of Silicon Motion’s recovery would come down to execution in the second half, demand recovery, and signs that the product pipeline could translate into actual dollars. Q2 2025 gave me some early answers, and there are a few levers here that I think investors should pay close attention to.

Client SSD: PCIe Gen5 ramp is real

The SM2508 controller, which is Silicon Motion’s flagship PCIe 5.0 product, now accounts for roughly 10% of client SSD revenue. That’s not just a technical milestone; it’s a signal that OEM adoption is happening ahead of schedule. I had penciled in a meaningful Gen5 mix closer to Q4 or even early 2026, but this early traction suggests ASP uplift and design win momentum are already feeding through. I think the outlook here is incredibly positive, especially with Gen5 SSDs becoming increasingly standard in high‑end laptops and gaming rigs, so this mix shift should continue to support SIMO’s margin expansion.

Enterprise SSD: MonTitan is finally ramping

This one’s been a slow burn. MonTitan always looked promising on paper (high‑performance, customizable, built for hyperscale), but in my view, the real question for investors was whether it would translate into the sort of revenue that rerates multiples. If we dig into the commentary from the Q2 earnings call, we can see that the quarter confirmed that multiple Tier‑1 customers are now actively ramping deployments. That’s a big deal because while it doesn’t make SIMO an enterprise heavyweight overnight, it validates the company’s strategy and opens the door to more predictable growth in FY26. I’ll be watching closely to see how this scales, especially in the context of BlueField DPU boot drive wins later this year.

eMMC/UFS: China handset cycle offers near‑term lift

I think this segment was the most surprising in terms of the upside. I didn’t focus too much on how it would factor into the bigger picture earlier, even though it’s clear that it can be a major contributor. Here, growth was a 40–45% sequential increase in eMMC/UFS revenue, albeit a lot of that was driven by higher restocking demand from Chinese smartphone OEMs. I’m not quite sure it will be a sustained growth engine, but it can definitely contribute to the topline and act as a useful buffer against SSD volatility.

The main takeaway for me is that it shows that SIMO’s controller portfolio is still relevant in midrange and entry‑level mobile platforms, and that is a big benefit because they tend to be less cyclical than flagship devices.

Automotive: Early traction, long runway

Automotive is still a small piece of the pie, but the signs are there that it will start scaling soon, so I think it’s worth flagging. Management says that the segment can start contributing as much as 10% of the company’s revenue over the next year or so, and that is backed up by a few key developments. First, SIMO’s ASPICE Level 3‑certified SSD controllers are now shipping into EV platforms, and the company is engaged with multiple automotive Tier 1s like Toyota, Honda, and BYD. These are big names that understand the market’s direction, especially in China.

Also, automotive storage demand is growing (your car’s infotainment, ADAS, and over‑the‑air updates all require reliable flash), and SIMO is positioning itself as a credible supplier. Right now, I’d file automotive as a non-material revenue driver, but given that it’s a long‑term optionality play, we can consider it by Q3 or Q4, when we have more solid numbers to work with in terms of the various products and pricing across the segment. That way, we can see the potential revenue added and how it could become more meaningful by 2026–2027, whether it can really hit that 10% number that management is so buoyed by.

Valuation & Outlook

Let’s be clear: I don’t think that SIMO is a cheap stock on an absolute basis, but it’s not at the level it should be relative to its trajectory. The market’s still treating it like a cyclical laggard at the same time that the fundamentals are quietly shifting toward secular growth.

What the multiples are telling me:

  • P/E compression is coming: The consensus EPS growth for FY26 is north of 40%. If that happens, SIMO’s forward P/E drops into the mid-teens, and you’re looking at a growth story trading at value multiples.

  • EV/EBITDA is elevated, but justified: The 16.6x forward multiple looks a bit rich until you factor in the Gen5 mix shift and MonTitan ramp-up over the next few quarters. They’re structural margin drivers, and going off the company’s projected revenue growth, I’m expecting EBITDA margin expansion of 300–400 bps over the next 18 months.

  • Dividend yield is quietly attractive: At 2.55%, SIMO’s yield compares very favorably to its sector multiples across the board. In my view, the yield is a signal of balance sheet strength and capital discipline, especially because the company has zero debt and $208M in cash, so there’s room to flex.

Also, when we compare SIMO to peers like DIOD (53x FWD P/E) or POWI (89x FWD P/E), SIMO’s 29x forward P/E looks reasonable, especially given its diversified product mix and improving margin profile. Of course, SIMO’s current revenue growth is more modest than either of those two, and it looks nearly inconsequential compared to SYNA, but its controller business is starting to show similar operating leverage, so the revenue upside is obvious.

I believe that if SIMO executes on Gen5 and MonTitan, we might see the stock well on its way to $95–100 by mid‑2026, especially if the Street starts to re-rate the enterprise SSD narrative.

Risks

Every thesis has its risks, and I’m not blind to the ones that exist for SIMO. I just believe they’re manageable or unlikely to have much of an effect, and that’s why I’m still bullish.

1. Client SSD demand softens post-Q4. The Gen5 ramp is real, but it’s also cyclical. If OEMs front-load purchases ahead of holiday builds, we could see a volume dip in Q1. To be clear, that’s not going to break my thesis, but it could pressure margins short-term. However, the ASP uplift from Gen5 should offset volume softness. Plus, SIMO’s exposure to gaming and other consumer laptops gives it a buffer most controller peers don’t have.

2. MonTitan fails to scale beyond initial deployments. Enterprise SSD is an exciting, big-play arena for SIMO, so if MonTitan stalls or we see hyperscaler adoption plateau, then the margin expansion story can lose steam. Fortunately, I don’t think this is likely to happen, given that early traction is coming from multiple Tier 1s, not just one anchor customer. And with BlueField boot drive wins in the pipeline, SIMO has a second wedge into enterprise flash. I’m watching the quarterly revenue mix closely.

3. Regulatory or geopolitical shocks. SIMO has a fair bit of exposure to China, both in its supply chain and the customer base, so any escalation in US-China trade tensions could disrupt some parts of its operations. On that front, though, the company is largely diversified across Korea, Taiwan, and the US, so it shouldn’t be a problem. Plus, the controller business is less exposed to export controls than full-stack NAND or DRAM players.

Final Thoughts

Silicon Motion is executing, plain and simple. The Gen5 ramp is on schedule, MonTitan is finally monetizing, mobile gave us tactical upside, and automotive is quietly gaining traction. We can also see that margins are expanding, guidance is firm, and the balance sheet is clean. The market’s still pricing this like a cyclical controller vendor, but the fundamentals are shifting. As such, I’m reiterating my Buy.

data.title: Lazard International Equity Select Portfolio Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Overview

  • International equity markets rose significantly during the second quarter, adding to the YTD lead vs. the US
  • International corporate profit growth remained resilient amid a volatile geopolitical environment
  • International currencies remained strong as the US Dollar experienced its worst first half of the year since 1973
  • The risk-on environment saw Small Caps outperform Large, and Growth outperform Value, with rates relatively stable
  • Quality continues to lag the market by historic amounts, a function of higher exposure to US revenue

International equities continued their strong run in 2025. The MSCI ACW ex-US and MSCI EAFE Indexes rose a strong 12% in the quarter, adding to their robust start to the year. Year to date, the MSCI ACW ex-US and MSCI EAFE Indexes have risen 18% and 19% respectively. International markets have outperformed the US by over 12% year to date. Strong international currencies have bolstered international outperformance. Prospects for fiscal stimulus and defense spending in Europe, coupled with geopolitical and budgetary uncertainty in the US, catalyzed a strong move in major international currencies versus the dollar with the Euro and Yen up 13% and 8% respectively year to date.

The quarter began with a significant increase in global tariffs and ended with a major military escalation in the Middle East. Very few investors would have anticipated a double-digit return for most global equity markets, but that’s what happened. Within a few days of the April 2 US tariff announcement, the S&P 500 Index and the MSCI EAFE Index fell roughly 14% and 11% respectively. At that point, the US announced a pause to the tariff implementation and markets quickly recovered. Earnings reporting season then ensued and international corporate profits demonstrated continued resilience, pushing markets higher. Earnings ultimately drive stock prices and earnings outside the US are rising.

Quality as a style continues to underperform not only the broader market but also both Growth and Value. Quality companies have seen their valuations derate significantly over the past few quarters. In fact, the degree of derating over the past year for developed markets has been the largest in the history of the MSCI indexes, exceeding the Global Financial Crisis in 2008/09.

Portfolio Review

During the second quarter, the Lazard International Equity Select Portfolio rose 11.3% (net of fees), lagging the MSCI ACW ex-US Index which rose 12.0%. The investment philosophy of the Lazard International Equity Platform is one of Relative Value where we contrast a company’s level and trajectory of financial productivity with its valuation. We believe that businesses with higher and more sustainable levels of financial productivity are deserving of higher valuations. We seek to buy these higher quality companies at relatively inexpensive valuations compared to the level of returns they generate.

When looking at the two fundamental factors we evaluate for each company, financial productivity and valuation, the Lazard International Equity Select Strategy is more biased toward quality and higher financial productivity. The premium to financial productivity compared to the benchmark is substantial and has posed a very significant headwind to relative performance during much of the post-COVID era as Quality significantly underperformed.

Looking back over nearly a quarter century, on a rolling 12-month basis, Quality has never lagged the market by more. Over this long period, the probability of Quality underperforming by this magnitude is over a 3 standard deviation event, or much less than a 1.0% chance. Over the long term, and inclusive of the past year, Quality has outperformed the market, on average, by over 2%, and we expect this to continue as we look forward.

Why is Quality underperforming? Over the past year, the valuation of the highest quintile of ROE within the MSCI ACW ex-US Index has fallen by nearly 20% relative to the benchmark, an amount second to the only comparative period which was the Global Financial Crisis.

We think valuations have derated due to two main factors: Higher inflation / interest rates, and US revenue exposure. Quality companies tend to be longer duration equities and can be more sensitive to the trajectory of longer-term rates. We think much of the move higher in rates is in the past and with it so too is this driver of the derating of Quality. In addition to higher rates / inflation as a driver of the derating, Quality companies tend to have higher revenue exposure to the US than the market. Because our portfolio is more skewed towards Quality, this effect also negatively impacted our performance. From a year-to-date attribution standpoint, companies in our portfolio with higher exposure to the US than the benchmark underperformed by roughly 482 basis points (bps) whereas those with lower exposure outperformed by 361 bps. With forward earnings growth expectations for US companies now converging with International earnings growth expectations, we also believe this driver of the derating is also behind us.

The Portfolio continues to be positioned in high quality compounders with a significant 30%+ premium in financial productivity.

We think many of the drivers of the Quality derating and resulting underperformance are behind us and are very excited about an improvement for Quality performance going forward. In the past quarter century, every time Quality underperformed the market by 5% or more, it outperformed in the subsequent period. We believe companies that can organically grow earnings while maintaining margins and profitability will be leaders going forward and therefore, we expect history to repeat.

Positives

Stock specific contributors to performance in the industrials sector included:

  • Diploma (1.2% weighting in the Portfolio) is domiciled in the UK and is an industrial distributor of small-ticket, mission critical operating expense items for broad categories of life sciences, seals, and controls. By utilizing a decentralized, autonomous business model, the company has been able to generate superior financial returns with a mid-teens ROIC. Historically, M&A has proven to be an excellent use of Diploma’s capital. The M&A strategy generally consists of small bolt-on deals, with limited bidding competition where the idea comes from the individual business units. Diploma is rarely the highest bidder but can assure the founder/seller that the business will be invested for growth, management usually stays on board and the business is left to run autonomously in the Diploma decentralized structure. Shares rose after the company reported first half results. Organic growth and EBITDA came in ahead of consensus expectations by 3% and 5% respectively and full year guidance was raised. These results are supportive of our investment thesis, and we added to the Portfolio’s position. We believe, over the mid-term, Diploma could grow 6-7%+ organically, plus an additional 5-10% via M&A (7% in H1), earn margins greater than 20% and a ROIC of ~15% with good resiliency in downturns.
  • MTU Aero Engines (2.1% weighting) is a German aerospace engine supplier. Outside of engine manufacturing, the company’s other main source of revenue comes from repairing other types of engines at its maintenance, repair, and overhaul (MRO) shops. Spare parts are extremely high margin and engine maintenance is a regulated activity that must be performed after a certain number of engine cycles, or take-offs and landings. We believe MTU offers the purest exposure to the aftermarket space, directly through its OEM division (engines and spare parts) and secondarily through its MRO segment. Shares rose after the company reported first quarter results. Sales and earnings came in ahead of consensus expectations, driven by strong aftermarket performance, and free cash flow also came in ahead of consensus expectations. These results are supportive of our investment thesis, and we maintained the Portfolio’s position.

Lower-than-index exposure to the underperforming energy sector positively contributed to relative returns.

Negatives

Companies with relatively higher US revenue exposure were among the largest detractors from performance.

  • Shares of Ireland-based global Insurance broker Aon (2.1% weighting) declined after the company reported slightly weaker-than-expected first quarter results. While the operational performance was largely as expected, investors sold off the shares as foreign exchange headwinds impacted reported sales leading to a slightly lower-than-expected earnings per share in the quarter. Favorably, management reiterated full-year guidance for mid-single-digit or greater organic growth and expectation for ~80 to 90 basis points (bps) of year-over-year (Y/Y) operating margin expansion towards ~32.3-32.4% (up from ~31.5% in 2024). We believe the share price reaction was overdone and the long-term earnings power of the company remains intact. As a broker and an asset-light company in a fairly consolidated industry, Aon generates strong returns on capital which we believe will be sustained due to strong pricing power and cash flow generation. We believe the earnings power, rather than quarter to quarter changes in top line growth rates, will drive the shares long term. We believe the stock remains inexpensive trading on a ~17.5x 2026E PE for a firm that we believe will deliver mid-to-high single-digit top-line growth and low-double-digit adjusted EPS growth over the medium-term in a secularly growing market. We maintained the Portfolio’s position.
  • Domiciled in Switzerland, Chubb (0.9% weighting) is one of the world’s most prominent and reputable insurance companies. Shares declined after the company reported mixed first quarter results where the continuation of better-than-expected strong underwriting performance across all segments was offset by the continuation of lighter-than-expected Net Premiums Written (NPW) and Net Premiums Earned (NPE). We maintained the Portfolio’s position and continue to monitor the situation.
  • Based in Hong Kong, Techtronic (1.2% weighting) is a leading global designer, manufacturer, and marketer of home improvement products. The majority of Techtronic’s revenue is derived from its power tools business which operates under the brands Milwaukee (professional power tools) and Ryobi (consumer power tools). Shares underperformed on tariff-related headwinds. We added to the Portfolio’s position, taking advantage of share price weakness.

Outlook

  • We believe heightened geopolitical uncertainty is likely to persist, and may result in slower investment and consumption
  • Central bank options could be limited by increasing fiscal deficits
  • We believe the drivers of US outperformance over the past several years may be diminishing Large International valuation discount and similar earnings growth vs. US suggest more upside following strong first half returns
  • In our view, historic quality underperformance presents a significant opportunity for outperformance going forward
  • Critical shift in investor sentiment should continue to benefit international markets and relative value investing

Quality as a style has outperformed the market over time. Yet, over the past year, Quality has underperformed significantly. This was due to the derating of Quality valuations resulting from higher rates as well as more exposure to US revenue. We think these negative effects on Quality are likely in the past and the historic pullback in valuations could offer a compelling opportunity to invest in the long-term compounding of the earnings of companies with high financial productivity at relatively attractive valuations.

We believe companies with stronger pricing power are more equipped for the coming environment. They can more easily pass along higher costs to their customers without seeing demand deteriorate and will be more likely to maintain their margins. Companies with the widest moats, the highest financial productivity and the opportunity to reinvest for growth should resume leadership.

The topic of tariffs continued to be top of mind in the second quarter. The US’s pause on tariff implementations breathed a sigh of relief into global equity markets and perhaps pulled in a little demand as well. Company managements continue to determine what tariffs mean for their businesses – and if they will even be implemented at all. From sales demand, to pricing, to margins – this new policy affects many facets of investment planning and decision making. In the near term, we believe management teams will need to wait to see how the situation sorts out. The initial inventory buildup ahead of potential tariff implementation may result in sales and profits being negatively impacted. We continue to speak with all of our companies trying to learn how these new policies will impact their businesses. Stock selection will be critical to sorting out winners and losers.

We expect central bank policy to continue to be top of mind. As of the end of the second quarter, the fixed income market is expecting the Federal Reserve and European Central bank to cut rates 50 basis points(bps) and 25 bps respectively by the end of 2025. Further accommodation is expected in 2026. With unemployment still reasonably low and the prospect for increasing fiscal deficits, the pace and aggressiveness of accommodation relative to expectations will likely be an area of focus for investors.

Since the Global Financial Crisis, global equity market returns have been led by the US. Initially that had much to do with how quickly US companies recovered from the GFC, but more recently the outperformance of US equities has been largely attributable to three key factors: Dollar strength, the concentration and outsized impact of the Magnificent 7, and these two factors combined to help drive the valuation of the US market to extraordinary levels. All three of these factors are reversing or diminishing and we think International equities can continue to outperform although it is unlikely to the degree we have seen in the first half of 2025.

With the valuation discount of International equities still near all-time wide levels, we think International equities can continue to outperform.
